message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:p>
                  We aimed to clarify if the infection and death rate by COVID-19 differ among gender in the top 50 countries with the highest death rates. Also, we investigated if secondary variables such as HDI, number of hospital beds, average age, temperature, percentage of elderly, smoker and obesity are contributing to the variability observed among countries. Meta-analyses and meta-regressions approaches were applied to official public data reported by the Word Health Organization and governments until May, 2020. A random effect model was used for the meta-analysis and heterogeneity was calculated by I
                  <jats:sup>2</jats:sup>
                  statistic. There was not significative difference between men and women to be infected by COVID-19 (
                  <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                  = 0.42), though a significative difference was observed for death rate (
                  <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                  < 0.0001). High heterogeneity was observed among countries. For both infection and death rates this variability was mainly explained by the HDI (42.3% and 54.2%), average age (40.9% and 40.3%) and temperature (30.1% and 39.3%). Man are dying more than women around the word by COVID-19. Countries with highest HDI present less difference between sexes. These results reinforce that public politics promoting social isolation, health care and general well-being of the population are key factors in combating COVID-19.
                </jats:p>
title: Are men dying more than women by COVID-19?

reference.article-title: Sex difference and smoking predisposition in patients with COVID-19
